title: Chapter 17: The FBI Circle
content: The fluorescent lights in the federal building on Los Angeles Street buzzed at a frequency designed to keep people awake — or drive them slowly insane. Special Agent Diana Reyes, forty-two, had been listening to it for eight months and still couldn't decide which.

The dossier on her desk was thick enough to strain the manila folder: PHANTOM LEDGER — CASE #2024-CF-0847. She'd read it so many times the tab was soft as cloth. The ICO had raised $2.3 billion. Two point three billion, with a B. The SEC had flagged irregularities in token distribution — wash trading, circular flows, wallets that appeared and vanished like ghosts. And then the whistleblower, inside the company, feeding documents through a sealed channel.
She sipped coffee from a paper cup. Cold. It had been cold for an hour. She sipped it anyway.
The case was building. But "building" and "built" were different verbs, and Diana Reyes was a woman who preferred the past tense.
I find out about the raid the way I find out about everything now — from Jax, after the fact, in a room where the light is low and her voice is lower.

"They came at nine," she says. She's standing at the window of the apartment, arms crossed, silhouetted against a sky that can't decide between gray and gold. "Fifteen agents. Two subpoenas. One very rattled general counsel."
"Reyes?"
"Special Agent Diana Reyes." Jax turns from the window. Her mouth curves, but it's not a smile. "She's good. Methodical. She interviewed half the floor before lunch."
My stomach drops. "The servers—"
"The real servers?" Jax shakes her head. "Decentralized mesh. Distributed across eleven jurisdictions. No single warrant touches them. She knows that. That's what's making her so damn thorough."

I can picture it — the Phantom Ledger office in SOMA, the glass walls that once looked like the future, now full of people in federal jackets carrying boxes. The leather briefcases, the badges on lanyards, the way fluorescent light turns everyone's skin the color of old paper. Jax's employees sitting at their standing desks, pretending to cooperate while their hearts hammer against their ribs.
"Did they take anything useful?" I ask.
Jax shrugs. "Correspondence. Internal memos. The usual theater. Enough to show a judge they're serious. Not enough to show a jury I'm guilty." She pauses. "Yet."
That word hangs between us like smoke.
Jax's lawyer is named Harriet Vance.
"No relation," Jax tells me before the meeting, and laughs — a real laugh, the first one I've heard in days. "I know. I asked her the same thing."

Harriet Vance operates out of a corner office in a building that screams old money and older threats. Dark wood paneling. Leather chairs that cost more than my entire documentary budget. Windows overlooking the Embarcadero. The air smells like sandalwood and billable hours.

I'm not supposed to be in this meeting. I know that. But Jax pulled me inside like it was the most natural thing in the world, and Harriet didn't blink, so here I am, sitting in a chair that swallows me, holding a cup of coffee I haven't touched, watching the woman I love get told how many years she might spend in prison.

"The FBI has enough for a warrant," Harriet says. She's brusque, blunt, the kind of lawyer who weaponizes directness. Gray hair pulled tight, reading glasses on a chain, a posture that suggests she's never once in her life been impressed. "But not enough for conviction. Yet."
That word again. Yet. The ticking beneath every sentence.
"If they find the shadow ledger," Harriet continues, "you're looking at wire fraud, securities fraud, and potentially racketeering. That's fifteen to twenty."
The numbers land like stones in water. Fifteen years. Twenty years. Jax Vance, who moves through the world like light through crystal, locked in a room with no windows for two decades.
Jax doesn't flinch. "Then we make sure they don't find it."
Harriet removes her glasses. She looks at Jax the way you look at a patient who's refusing treatment — with respect, and with the certainty that respect won't be enough.
"That's not a legal strategy," she says. "That's a prayer."
"Then pray."
Harriet doesn't laugh. Neither do I.
That night, Jax starts planning her exit.

I don't realize what she's doing at first. She's on her laptop, multiple windows open — flight routes, property listings in countries without extradition treaties, shell company structures that make my eyes cross. I think she's working on Phantom Ledger business until I see a tab titled "DUAL CITIZENSHIP — REQUIREMENTS."
"Jax."
She doesn't look up. "Hmm?"
"What are you doing?"
She closes the laptop. Slowly. The way you close something when you know the conversation that follows will be harder than whatever's on the screen.
"Preparing," she says.
"For what?"
"For the end."
The word sits on the hardwood floor between us like something dropped and broken. The end. Of the company. Of the life she built. Of us, maybe, if I'm not brave enough to follow.
I sit down across from her. My hands are shaking. I press them flat against my thighs.
"You're leaving," I say. Not a question.

"I'm planning to leave. There's a difference." She reaches across and takes one of my hands. Her fingers are warm and steady. "There's a timeline now. Weeks, not months. If I stay ahead of it, I can be gone before they have enough to indict."
"Where?"

"Somewhere they can't reach. Somewhere the long arm of the FBI turns into a stub." A ghost of a smile. "I've been building exit routes since the beginning, Maya. I just never thought I'd have to use them."
I want to ask: alone? But the word won't come out. It's too big, too final, too much like a door closing.
Jax answers it anyway.
"Vivienne," she says. "I need Vivienne. I can't disappear alone. I won't." Her grip on my hand tightens. "What's the point of escaping if she's not on the other side?"

She means Vivienne. She says Vivienne's name. But she's looking at me, and her eyes are bright with something that isn't fear, and I realize — with a lurch that rearranges everything inside my chest — that she means me too. She's talking about Vivienne and she's talking about me. The people she won't leave behind. The people who make freedom worth having.
What's the point of escaping if she's not on the other side?
It's the most romantic thing anyone has ever said to me, and it breaks my heart.
Three days later, I'm editing footage when the realization hits me like a freight train.
I am evidence.

Not metaphorically. Literally. Every frame I've shot — Jax in the office, Jax on the yacht, Jax talking about the shadow ledger, Jax laughing in green laser light — all of it is discoverable. If the FBI subpoenas my footage, I become a witness for the prosecution. My documentary, the thing I've poured two years of my life into, becomes the rope they use to hang the woman I love.
I find Jax on the rooftop terrace, staring at the city like she's memorizing it. Like she's already saying goodbye.
"The FBI could subpoena my footage," I say. No preamble. No soft landing.
She doesn't turn around. "Yes."
"If they do, I'm a witness against you."
"Yes."
"I've documented everything, Jax. Everything. The yacht. The green code. The conversations about—"
"I know what you've documented." She turns now. Her face is calm. Too calm. The kind of calm that takes effort. "I've always known."
"Then what do we do?"

She crosses the terrace to me. Takes both my hands. The city sprawls behind her, all that light and steel and ambition, and she looks at me like I'm the only solid thing in a world that's liquefying under our feet.
"They can't subpoena what doesn't exist."
The words take a moment to land. When they do, my throat closes.
"You're asking me to destroy my documentary."
"I'm asking you to choose."
Her voice is gentle but unyielding. No soft-pedaling. No escape hatch. She's giving me the respect of a real decision — the kind that costs something no matter which way you go.

My documentary. Two years of work. The project that was supposed to be my breakthrough, my calling card, my proof that Maya Chen could see the world clearly and make other people see it too. Every interview. Every intimate moment. Every stolen second of green light on dark water.
Or Jax.
I pull my hands away. Not cruelly — I just need to feel my own weight for a moment. To stand on my own feet in the gravity of this choice.
"You're asking me to burn my future," I say. "For a chance at ours."
"I'm asking you to decide which future matters more."

The breeze off the bay carries the smell of salt and diesel and someone's late-night cooking. A siren wails in the distance — an ambulance, probably, or the police. In this city, it's hard to tell the difference between rescue and arrest.
I don't answer her. Not yet. Because the answer is already forming inside me, clear and terrifying, and once I say it out loud, there's no taking it back.
In a federal building on Los Angeles Street, the fluorescent lights still buzz.

Special Agent Diana Reyes sits alone in her office. The coffee cup on her desk is empty, a brown ring drying at the bottom. The dossier is closed. The work is on the wall now — a cork board bristling with photos, documents, printouts, and thread.

She picks up a photograph. Jax Vance, caught by a paparazzo at some tech gala — sharp jaw, sharper eyes, hair swept back, mouth curved in a smile that suggests she knows something you don't. Reyes pins it to the center of the board.
Then she picks up a second photograph.

This one was harder to get. It's grainy, shot from distance, clearly surveillance — a yacht at night, moored off the coast, green laser light fracturing across the water like bioluminescence. The yacht where, according to her sources, Phantom Ledger's true operations were conducted. The yacht that exists in no official company paperwork. The yacht that glows green.
She pins it next to Jax's photo.

Then she takes a length of red string — she still uses string, her colleagues think it's quaint, she thinks it works — and connects the two photographs. Jax to the yacht. The founder to the green light.
She writes on a sticky note, blue ink, precise handwriting:
*Follow the light.*
She presses it to the board beneath the string.

The investigation is eight months old. The whistleblower gave her documents. The SEC gave her numbers. But the yacht — the green laser yacht — that came from somewhere else. Someone else. Someone who knows about the code, the shadow system, the thing that hums beneath Phantom Ledger's surface like electricity through water.
Someone is feeding Diana Reyes more than documents. Someone is feeding her the shape of the machine.

She turns off the desk lamp. The fluorescent ceiling light buzzes on, casting everything in that flat, institutional glow that flattens shadows and sharpens edges. The cork board looks almost alive in it — a web of connections, a circle closing.
On the wall, Jax Vance smiles out at the agent who's coming for her, and the green light glows like a heartbeat, and the red string stretches taut between them.
The clock is ticking. And someone, somewhere, is holding the key.
